name: SeaORM
what_it_does: Async ORM for Rust built on top of SQLx and sea-query. SeaORM bridges the gap between Diesel (sync, query-builder-heavy) and SQLx (async, raw SQL). It provides Active Record-style entity models, async CRUD operations, eager loading (find_with_related), migrations via sea-schema, and supports all major databases. The go-to async ORM for Rust web applications.
use_cases: Build async Rust web applications with ORM-style entity models — define entities with derive macros, get CRUD for free without raw SQL
Implement complex database queries with SeaORM's query builder for agent data persistence layers in async Tokio applications
Use eager loading (find_with_related) to fetch parent+children entities in two optimized queries — avoiding N+1 for agent list operations
Manage database schema with sea-orm-migration — version-controlled async migrations that run on application startup
Test agent database layers with SeaORM's mock interface — mock_conn returns predefined results without a real database
not_for: Synchronous applications — SeaORM is async-first; sync contexts require blocking workarounds
Teams preferring raw SQL — SQLx offers compile-time SQL verification without ORM abstraction overhead
Applications where Diesel's compile-time query checking provides value — SeaORM's runtime query building trades compile-time safety for ergonomics
best_when: You're building an async Rust application (Tokio/Axum/Actix) and want ORM ergonomics (entity models, relationships, migrations) without the synchronous constraint of Diesel.
avoid_when: You need compile-time SQL validation — SQLx is better for catching SQL errors at build time. SeaORM's query builder validates at runtime.

auth.notes: Library — no external auth. Database credentials via connection string.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Fully open source — MIT/Apache 2.0 dual licensed
pricing.notes: MIT/Apache 2.0 dual-licensed Rust crate. Active development by SeaQL organization.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — self-hosted. Built on SQLx connection pools.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Errors use DbErr enum with variants for connection, query, type conversion failures. Error messages include SQL context when available. Less descriptive than Diesel's compile-time errors.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Read operations idempotent. Upsert via Entity::insert().on_conflict(). Transactions via db.transaction() closure for atomic retries.
known_agent_gotchas: Entity code generation from existing schema uses sea-orm-cli — running generate entity on a live database overwrites custom entity modifications; maintain entities manually after initial generation
find_with_related() issues exactly 2 queries (parent + children) — not a JOIN — so very large result sets may be slower than a manual JOIN query for agent bulk operations
ActiveModel fields use ActiveValue::Set vs ActiveValue::NotSet to distinguish set-and-update from leave-unchanged — forgetting NotSet may unintentionally overwrite fields with None
Migrations in SeaORM are Rust code (not SQL files) — the migration table tracks executed migrations but Rust code changes don't auto-detect schema drift the way Diesel's schema.rs does
SeaORM's mock interface returns predefined JSON results — mock responses must match exact column order and types or tests fail with confusing deserialization errors
Version compatibility between sea-orm, sea-query, and sea-schema crates is strict — mismatched versions cause trait implementation errors at compile time that are hard to diagnose

security_score_components.security_notes: Parameterized queries via SQLx prevent SQL injection. Rust type safety. No credential management — application responsibility.
